meta.lessonTitle: Let AI Do the Repetitive Work

steps.title: Welcome to Day 15
steps.body: Some tasks eat your time every single day — replying to the same kinds of emails, scheduling meetings, sending reminders. Today you will learn how to hand those tasks to AI so they happen on their own.

steps.title: What is automation?
steps.body: Automation means setting up a chain of steps that run without you. You describe the trigger and the action once — then it just happens. Think of it like a domino: you push one tile and the rest fall in sequence.

steps.title: Marcus's Monday mornings
steps.body: Every Monday Marcus spent 45 minutes on the same tasks: replying to the weekly status email, sending a meeting reminder to his team, and logging the week's goals in a doc. He set up three automations and got those 45 minutes back. Every week.

steps.title: The two parts of an automation
steps.body: Every automation has two parts: a TRIGGER (what starts it) and an ACTION (what happens next). Example — Trigger: you receive an email with the word 'invoice'. Action: AI drafts a reply and labels the email. That is it. Trigger then action.

steps.title: Automation ideas for daily life
steps.body: Here are tasks you can automate today: drafting replies to routine emails, sending weekly team check-in reminders, scheduling recurring meetings, logging daily tasks to a doc, summarizing long email threads, flagging urgent messages.

steps.title: An automation prompt
steps.prompt: I get a lot of emails from clients asking for a status update on their project. Write me a short reply template I can use or automate. The reply should: acknowledge their question, tell them I will follow up within 24 hours, and sound friendly but professional. Keep it under 5 sentences.

steps.title: Tools that connect AI to your apps
steps.body: You do not need to code anything. Tools like Zapier and Make act as connectors. You pick an app (like Gmail or Google Calendar), pick a trigger, pick an action, and the tool does the wiring for you. Think of them as 'if this, then that' machines.

steps.title: How a connector tool works
steps.body: Imagine you use Gmail and Google Calendar. You set up a rule: 'When I receive a meeting invite, automatically add it to my calendar and send the organizer a quick confirmation.' You set it up once in a connector tool. From then on it runs silently in the background.

steps.title: Automation check
steps.question: What are the two essential parts of any automation?
feedback: Not quite — Q&A is how you talk to AI, not how automations are structured.
Exactly right. A trigger starts the chain and an action is what happens next.
Those are for logging in, not for automations.
Prompts and templates are helpful, but automations are built on triggers and actions.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: You need programming skills to set up most simple AI automations.
feedback: Correct! Most automation tools today use simple point-and-click interfaces. No coding required.
Actually, modern automation tools like Zapier and Make are designed for non-technical users.

steps.title: Good for automation?
steps.question: Send a thank-you email every time someone fills out my contact form on my website.
feedback: Yes! This is a perfect automation — same trigger, same action, every time.
Exactly. Repetitive, predictable tasks like this are ideal candidates for automation.

steps.title: Good for automation?
steps.question: Automatically decide whether to fire an employee based on their performance data.
feedback: Right to be cautious. Decisions that affect people's lives need human judgment, not automation.
Correct. Automation handles routine tasks. High-stakes people decisions always need a human.

steps.question: In an automation, the event that starts the chain is called the ______.
feedback: The correct word is 'trigger' — it is the event that kicks everything off.
Exactly right. The trigger is what starts the automation running.

steps.title: Which task is best for automation?
steps.question: Which of these tasks is the best candidate for automation?
options: Writing a heartfelt birthday message for your best friend
Sending the same weekly reminder to your team every Monday at 9am
Deciding how to respond to a complaint from an unhappy client
Choosing which job offer to accept
feedback: Personal messages benefit from your own voice and care — not a great fit for full automation.
Perfect. It is the same action, same time, every week — exactly what automation is built for.
Client complaints need judgment and empathy, which humans do better.
Major life decisions always need your own thinking, not an automated system.

steps.title: Design your first automation

fields.label: What repetitive task takes up your time?
What event should start the automation?
fields.placeholder: when I receive an email with the word 'status'

fields.label: What should happen automatically?
fields.placeholder: send a template reply saying I will follow up in 24 hours

steps.template: Help me design an automation for this repetitive task: {{repetitive_task}}. The trigger should be: {{trigger}}. The desired action: {{action}}. Give me: (1) a simple plain-language description of the automation, (2) a draft of any text or message it would send, and (3) which free tool I could use to set it up (Zapier, Make, or a built-in app feature).

questions.label: What is one repetitive task you do every week that you wish would just handle itself?
How ready do you feel to try setting up a simple automation?
options: Interested but want to learn more first
A little nervous but open to it

questions.question: What does a connector tool like Zapier or Make do?
options: It links your apps together so they can trigger actions automatically
It stores all your AI prompts

questions.question: Automations work best for tasks that are the same every time they happen.
The result that an automation produces is called the ______.

steps.title: Day 15 complete — Automation Starter
steps.body: You now understand what automation is, how triggers and actions work, and which tasks are worth automating. Tomorrow: you will compare the top AI tools so you always know which one to reach for.
